children.value: This tutorial will guide you through the whole course in case you decide to work on the assignments locally on your machine. It covers everything from accepting the assignment to submitting your work, including troubleshooting common issues.

It is a good idea do bookmark this guide for easy reference as you work through the course. You will need to refer back to it multiple times, especially in the beginning when you are setting up your environment and getting familiar with the tools.
Before you start, install the following tools:
Version control to submit your work
Runs the database on your machine
VSCode extension to run SQL queries
as your terminal for all commands in this guide.
Click the invitation link your instructor shared
Wait a few seconds, then refresh the page
Click the link to your newly created repository
Create a virtual environment so the required packages don’t conflict with anything else on your machine:

python3 -m venv venv
source venv/bin/activate
pip install -r requirements.txt

python -m venv venv
venv\Scripts\activate
pip install -r requirements.txt

at the start of your terminal prompt,
confirming the environment is active.
You need to activate the virtual environment every time you open a new terminal window before running any Python commands.
Make sure Docker Desktop is open and running, then:

You will see PostgreSQL starting up in the terminal.
When you see a line like:
database system is ready to accept connections

The database is ready. Leave this terminal window open —
closing it stops the database.

If you want to run the database in the background
so you can keep using the same terminal window, use:
Step 5 — Connect VSCode to the Database

children.label: Step 5 — Connect VSCode to the Database

children.value:
The repository includes a pre-configured database connection.
To use it:

in the VSCode left sidebar
(looks like a database cylinder)
, you should see a connection already listed
A green checkmark confirms you are connected
If the connection does not appear automatically:

SQLTools sidebar
→ Add new connection
→ PostgreSQL
→ Fill in:
    Host:     localhost
    Port:     5432
    Database: [DATABASE NAME]
    Username: admin
    Password: admin
→ Save connection
Before writing queries, take a moment to understand the data.

In the SQLTools sidebar, expand your connection to see the tables.
Click any table to see its columns.
You can also run a quick exploration query — open any

file,
type the following, and press the

-- See all tables
SELECT table_name
FROM information_schema.tables
WHERE table_schema = 'public';

-- Preview a table (replace 'students' with any table name)
SELECT * FROM students LIMIT 5;
Open a question file. You will see something like this:

-- ============================================================
-- Question 1 (10 points)
-- List the name and GPA of all students with a GPA above 3.5
-- Order results by GPA descending
--
-- Expected columns: name, gpa
-- ============================================================

-- Write your query below:
Write your query below the comment block:

-- Write your query below:
SELECT name, gpa
FROM students
WHERE gpa > 3.5
ORDER BY gpa DESC;
To run the query and see results, click the

button
in the top right of the editor, or press
Results appear in a table at the bottom of the screen.

Before submitting, run the grader to see how many questions
you have correct:

✅ q1: correct   (10 pts)
❌ q2: incorrect  (0 pts)
✅ q3: correct   (10 pts)
💥 q4: error — syntax error at or near "FORM"  (0 pts)
⬜ q5: empty file  (0 pts)

Score: 20/50

again until you are happy
with your score.
When you are ready to submit:

git add .
git commit -m "completed homework"
git push
GitHub Actions will automatically run the grader on your submission.

Go to your repo on GitHub
→ Click the Actions tab
→ Click the most recent workflow run
→ See ✅ or ❌ per question and your total score

You can push as many times as you want before the deadline.
Each push triggers a new grading run.

If you accidentally modify the data (with INSERT, UPDATE, or DELETE)
and want to start fresh:

docker compose down -v
docker compose up

flag removes all data. The database will be recreated
from scratch with the original seed data.

Make sure Docker Desktop application is open and running
before running
Cannot connect to the database in SQLTools

children.label: Cannot connect to the database in SQLTools

children.value: Make sure the database is running first:
Then try connecting again in SQLTools.

Make sure your virtual environment is activated — you should
see
in your terminal prompt. If not:
The database is not running. Start it with:

children.label: I get 💥 syntax error on a question

children.value:
There is a SQL syntax error in your query. Read the error message
carefully — it usually tells you exactly where the problem is.
Common mistakes:
I accidentally pushed with empty query files

children.label: I accidentally pushed with empty query files

children.value:
No problem — just write your queries and push again.
Each push creates a new grading run.
docker compose down -v && docker compose up -d
git add . && git commit -m "msg" && git push
on your repo to ask your instructor questions
Bring specific error messages to office hours or tutoring sessions
on GitHub for detailed grading logs
